title: CICS Basics
description: Learn the fundamentals of CICS application development and online transaction processing.

theory.markdown:
## Welcome to CICS Basics

### What CICS is (and why it exists)
CICS (Customer Information Control System) is IBM’s transaction processing and application runtime environment for online workloads on IBM Z. It sits between your application programs and the z/OS platform services, providing a controlled way to run high‑volume, low‑latency transactions, manage resources, and maintain system integrity.

In a typical setup, **a user request starts a transaction**. CICS then **creates a task** to run that transaction instance and gives control to the program(s) that implement the business logic. When the program finishes its short unit of work, it returns control back to CICS rather than “owning” the machine the way a standalone batch program does.

### Key terms you must separate early
- **Transaction**: the requested unit of online processing (identified by a TRANSID).
- **Task**: the running instance that CICS creates to process one transaction request.
- **Program**: the application code that gets control under CICS to handle that request.

This distinction becomes essential later when you debug ("Which transaction ran?" vs "Which program failed?" vs "What data was passed?").

### How programs talk to CICS: EXEC CICS
CICS application programs request services by issuing **`EXEC CICS`** commands. Those commands are the API to:
- send/receive terminal data
- access files/queues
- link or transfer control to other programs
- handle syncpoints (units of work)

For your first exercise, the simplest useful command is **`EXEC CICS SEND TEXT`**. It demonstrates the *shape* of a CICS request without introducing maps or file control.

### Why the first exercise uses SEND TEXT
It teaches three foundational habits:
1. Your program is **running inside** a managed online environment.
2. Output is done via **CICS services** (not “normal” standalone I/O).
3. The program should do **one small piece of work** and then return control cleanly.

---

### References (academic/official)
1. IBM Documentation, *What you need to know about CICS* (CICS fundamentals, transactions/tasks, service managers). `https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/cics-ts/5.6?topic=cics-what-you-need-know-about`
2. IBM Redbooks, *CICS Transaction Server from Start to Finish (SG24-7952)* (introductory CICS concepts, terminology). `https://www.redbooks.ibm.com/redbooks/pdfs/sg247952.pdf`
3. IBM Documentation (PDF), *Developing CICS Applications* (EXEC CICS application programming overview). `https://www.ibm.com/docs/SSGMCP_5.4.0/pdf/application-programming_pdf.pdf`
theory.content:
Welcome to CICS Basics

CICS, short for Customer Information Control System, is IBM's transaction processing system for online applications on the mainframe. It is widely used in banking, insurance, government, and other large business environments where many users need fast and reliable access to data at the same time. Unlike a simple batch program, a CICS application runs as part of an online transaction environment in which requests come in, programs process them quickly, and control is returned to CICS.

The purpose of this chapter is to introduce the basic idea of what CICS is, how a CICS program fits into the online environment, and how simple output can be sent back to a terminal. Before you can understand files, maps, COMMAREAs, or inter-program communication, you first need a solid picture of the transaction model itself.

What CICS Does

At its core, CICS manages online transactions. A user action, terminal input, or program request starts a transaction. CICS then gives control to the appropriate application program, provides access to resources such as files or queues, and handles the execution environment. When the work is done, the program returns control to CICS instead of behaving like a normal standalone program that simply starts and ends under its own full control.

This distinction is very important. In normal standalone programming, you often think in terms of a single program flow from beginning to end. In CICS, you think in terms of short units of work called transactions. Each transaction is expected to do its work quickly, use CICS services correctly, and then give control back. That is one of the defining ideas learners need to understand from the beginning.

Transactions, Tasks, and Programs

A transaction is the logical unit of online work that a user or system requests. When that transaction runs, CICS creates a task and loads or transfers control to the application program that handles the request. In practical terms, many beginners use the words too loosely, so it helps to separate them clearly: the transaction is what was requested, the task is the running instance managed by CICS, and the program is the application logic being executed.

Understanding this relationship helps with both development and debugging. If something goes wrong, you often need to know which transaction was started, which program was executed, and what input data the task received. That is why CICS training starts with the environment and not just with syntax.

Using EXEC CICS Commands

Application programs interact with CICS through EXEC CICS commands. These commands request services from CICS, such as sending text to a terminal, receiving input, reading a file, linking to another program, or writing to a queue. In other words, CICS programming is not just about language statements. It is about combining your language logic with CICS service requests.

In this first chapter, the simplest useful command is EXEC CICS SEND TEXT. It allows a program to send a message back to the terminal. This is a good starting point because it shows the basic shape of a CICS request without yet introducing more complex ideas like maps or files.

Why the First Exercise Uses SEND TEXT

The exercise for this chapter asks the learner to send a message to the terminal. That may seem simple, but it teaches several important ideas at once. First, the program is running in a CICS environment. Second, it uses EXEC CICS instead of normal standalone output statements. Third, the program returns control cleanly after doing one small piece of work. That is exactly the kind of mental model a beginner should develop for online transaction programs.

By the end of the chapter, the learner should understand that CICS programs are part of a managed runtime environment, that transactions are short and focused, and that CICS commands are used to communicate with terminals and other resources. These ideas are enough to support both the exercise and the quiz questions in this chapter.

What You Need for the Exercise

  • Know that CICS is an online transaction processing system
  • Understand that a transaction runs as a task under CICS control
  • Know that programs request CICS services with EXEC CICS
  • Use EXEC CICS SEND TEXT to send a terminal message
  • Return control cleanly after the work is complete
keyConcepts: Basic terminal output with SEND TEXT

exercise.instructions: Write a simple CICS program that sends the text 'WELCOME TO CICS' to the terminal.
exercise.hint: Declare a message variable and use EXEC CICS SEND TEXT FROM(...) LENGTH(...).

questions.question: What is the main purpose of CICS?
How do application programs request services from CICS?
What does a transaction represent in CICS?
options: A logical unit of online work

questions.question: Which command is commonly used to send a simple message to a terminal?
After a CICS program completes its work, what should it do?

theory.markdown:
## Core concepts: control flow + COMMAREA

### Why CICS apps are rarely one big program
In real CICS systems, a single transaction commonly involves multiple programs. CICS provides **program-control** services so you can structure applications as cooperating modules instead of a monolith. This improves maintainability and supports reuse, but it also means you must treat program-to-program boundaries like an interface.

### COMMAREA (DFHCOMMAREA): the classic interface contract
A COMMAREA is a region of data passed between programs. The key idea is not “memory sharing”; it is **an agreed data contract**.

Practical implications:
- The **sender and receiver must agree** on layout and meaning.
- The caller must pass the **correct length**.
- The callee must treat the input as **untrusted until validated** (especially in online flows).

A simple first rule is to check what was actually received (e.g., length) before referencing fields. In CICS, misunderstandings about *length* and *layout* are a frequent cause of confusing defects.

### LINK vs XCTL vs RETURN
- **`EXEC CICS LINK`**: call another program and expect control to return.
- **`EXEC CICS XCTL`**: transfer control to another program and do **not** return.
- **`EXEC CICS RETURN`**: end the current program’s work under CICS control (optionally with data for a follow-on step).

Choosing correctly matters because it defines the runtime call chain and the state that is preserved.

---

### References (academic/official)
1. IBM Documentation (PDF), *Developing CICS Applications* (program-control concepts and EXEC CICS usage). `https://www.ibm.com/docs/SSGMCP_5.4.0/pdf/application-programming_pdf.pdf`
2. IBM Documentation, *What you need to know about CICS* (CICS facilities; transactions, tasks, program control). `https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/cics-ts/5.6?topic=cics-what-you-need-know-about`
theory.content:
Core Concepts in CICS

Once you understand that CICS is an online transaction environment, the next step is learning how programs pass control and data. This chapter focuses on three essential ideas: communication between programs, passing data with COMMAREA, and using core program-control commands such as LINK, XCTL, and RETURN. These are central concepts in real CICS development because online applications are rarely built as one giant program. Instead, they are typically divided into smaller programs that work together.

The exercise in this chapter is designed to make the learner comfortable with passing data through a COMMAREA and calling another program. That is one of the most practical foundational skills in CICS because so many real applications rely on one program collecting or validating data and another program continuing the work.

What a COMMAREA Is

COMMAREA, commonly referred to as DFHCOMMAREA in programs, is a communication area used to pass data from one program to another in CICS. It is especially important in program-to-program communication and when one transaction passes control along with some input data. Instead of using global variables or uncontrolled shared memory, CICS programs often exchange structured data through this defined communication area.

For learning purposes, it is best to think of the COMMAREA as a block of data with an agreed layout. The sending program fills it, passes it, and the receiving program reads it using the same structure definition. This works well only when both sides agree on the format and length. That is why COMMAREA size and structure matter so much in both development and debugging.

LINK, XCTL, and RETURN

EXEC CICS LINK is used when one program calls another program and expects control to return after the called program completes. This is similar in idea to calling a subprogram, although it happens under CICS control. EXEC CICS XCTL is different because it transfers control to another program and does not expect control to return to the current one. EXEC CICS RETURN ends the current task step and gives control back to CICS, optionally passing a COMMAREA for later use.

These three commands are fundamental because they define the control flow of many CICS applications. A learner should not just memorize the command names. The important thing is to understand the role of each one: LINK for call-and-return style processing, XCTL for transfer of control without return, and RETURN for ending the program's current work under CICS.

Why Length Matters

When passing a COMMAREA, it is not enough to pass the data itself. You must also pass the correct length. If the length is shorter than the receiver expects, the receiving program may not see all the required fields. If the layout is inconsistent, fields may be interpreted incorrectly. In real systems, many online bugs are caused not by difficult logic, but by mismatched assumptions about the data that was passed.

This is why learners should treat COMMAREA handling as a disciplined interface. The sender and receiver must agree on structure, field meaning, and total length. That idea supports both this chapter's exercise and many future debugging topics.

A Practical Program-to-Program Flow

A common flow in CICS is that one program gathers input or starts the transaction, places values into a COMMAREA, and then issues LINK to a second program. The second program uses the passed data to continue processing, perhaps checking values, performing business rules, or accessing files. Once it completes, control returns to the first program if LINK was used.

That flow is exactly the mental model needed for the chapter exercise. The learner needs to create a small COMMAREA, put text into it, and pass it to another program. Even though the example is simple, it introduces one of the most important structural concepts in CICS development.

What You Need for the Exercise

  • Understand that a COMMAREA is used to pass data between CICS programs
  • Know that sender and receiver must agree on layout and length
  • Use EXEC CICS LINK to call another program and return after it finishes
  • Know the difference between LINK, XCTL, and RETURN
  • Pass the correct COMMAREA length with the request

exercise.instructions: Create a CICS program that places a short message into a COMMAREA and calls another program with EXEC CICS LINK.
exercise.hint: Declare a small structure or character field, move a value into it, and pass it with COMMAREA(...) LENGTH(...).

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a COMMAREA in CICS?
Which command is used when one CICS program calls another and expects control to return?
How is XCTL different from LINK?
options: XCTL transfers control and does not return to the current program
XCTL is only for file access
XCTL is used only for terminal printing

questions.question: Why is COMMAREA length important?
options: Because CICS ignores the data itself
Because the receiver must know how much valid data was passed
Because it changes the transaction id

questions.question: What does EXEC CICS RETURN do?
options: Transfers control permanently to another program
Ends the current work and returns control to CICS

theory.markdown:
## Advanced topics: file access and response handling

### Why file I/O changes the mindset
Once you start reading and writing records under CICS, you’re no longer just learning syntax—you’re learning how to write **safe online logic**. In online systems, a command can fail for expected reasons (record not found, resource unavailable, invalid key, etc.). The professional habit is to **treat every resource request as conditional**.

### Reading a record (conceptually)
A typical file-read request provides:
- a file identifier
- a key (RIDFLD)
- a target buffer (INTO)

### RESP / RESP2: turning failures into controlled paths
CICS encourages application programs to capture outcomes using **`RESP`** and **`RESP2`**, so you can:
- distinguish normal completion from defined conditions
- branch to a safe, user-friendly response
- avoid continuing with uninitialized data

This is exactly what your exercise trains: handle “record not found” as a *designed* path, not as an accident.

### A practical rule
Before using any data returned by CICS:
1. check the command outcome
2. only then continue processing

---

### References (academic/official)
1. IBM Documentation (PDF), *Developing CICS Applications* (application programming model, data management, response handling). `https://www.ibm.com/docs/SSGMCP_5.4.0/pdf/application-programming_pdf.pdf`
theory.content:
Advanced Topics in CICS

This chapter introduces CICS topics that feel closer to real application work. The main focus is file access and response handling, because many business transactions exist to retrieve, update, or validate stored data. In addition to file access, this chapter introduces the idea of checking command outcomes carefully so that the program behaves safely when something goes wrong.

The exercise is still intentionally small: read a record and handle the case where the read does not succeed normally. The broader goal, however, is to teach the learner how real CICS programs interact with resources and why checking results is one of the most important habits in online programming.

Reading Data in CICS

A common CICS activity is reading a record from a file using EXEC CICS READ. The program identifies the file, supplies a key with RIDFLD, and provides an area such as INTO(...) where the returned data should be placed. This is central to business transactions because users often request information such as customer details, policy data, balances, or order records.

However, a CICS file read is not guaranteed to succeed just because the command was issued. The record may not exist, the resource may be unavailable, the key may be wrong, or another condition may prevent normal completion. That is why reading data in CICS always goes together with response checking.

Using RESP and RESP2

To handle the outcome of a CICS command properly, developers often use RESP and RESP2. These values tell the program whether the command completed normally or whether a known condition occurred. Instead of assuming success, the program can inspect the response and take a safe and meaningful action.

For example, if a READ does not find a record, the program can handle that case directly and send a message such as 'RECORD NOT FOUND' instead of continuing as if valid data had been returned. This is an important professional habit because many production problems happen when programs ignore response codes and continue with invalid assumptions.

Why Error Handling Matters in Online Systems

In online systems, users expect quick and correct responses. If the program fails silently or continues with bad data, the result may be a misleading screen, a broken transaction, or even corrupt business processing. Good CICS programming therefore includes not only the successful path but also the controlled handling of expected failure conditions.

This is why the theory in this chapter goes beyond simple syntax. The learner should understand that reading a file is only part of the task. The real skill is to read safely, check the result, and respond correctly depending on what happened. That idea directly supports both the exercise and the quiz in this chapter.

A First Step Toward Real CICS Application Logic

At this stage, the learner begins moving from simple demonstration programs into application-style logic. A real CICS program often receives input, reads data, checks whether the operation was successful, and then either continues processing or returns a controlled error message. Even though this chapter only covers a basic record read, the pattern is the same one used in much larger business systems.

Understanding this pattern also prepares the learner for later topics such as map processing, temporary storage queues, transient data, syncpoints, and debugging. All of those areas depend on the same professional discipline: use CICS services correctly, inspect outcomes, and code explicit handling paths.

What You Need for the Exercise

  • Use EXEC CICS READ to retrieve a file record
  • Provide the file name, key field, and target record area
  • Check the result with RESP and optionally RESP2
  • Handle non-normal outcomes instead of assuming success
  • Return or send a clear message when a record is not found

exercise.instructions: Write a CICS program that attempts to read a customer record and sends 'RECORD FOUND' if the read succeeds or 'RECORD NOT FOUND' if it does not.
exercise.hint: Use EXEC CICS READ with RESP and test the response before deciding which message to send.

questions.question: Which CICS command is commonly used to read a record from a file?
What is the purpose of RIDFLD in a CICS READ command?
options: It supplies the key used to identify the record

questions.question: Why should a program check RESP after a CICS command?
options: Because the program must know whether the command succeeded or failed
Because RESP changes the file layout

questions.question: What is the best practice when a requested record is not found?
options: Handle the condition and send a controlled message

questions.question: Why is response handling especially important in online systems?
options: Because users need correct and controlled results
Because online programs never access data
Because CICS does not support files
Because RESP is only for debugging
